narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path despite the challenges many face, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from past students—like their early and later school grades, LSAT scores, undergraduate GPAs, family income levels, and whether they plan full-time studies—to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you, it estimated a 54% probability of failing the bar exam, which crossed the threshold that triggered the rejection to protect the school's outcomes. The biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your LSAT score and undergraduate GPA, both of which were notably lower than what's typical for students who succeed—your LSAT at 23 compared to an average of about 37, and your GPA at 2.3 versus the usual 3.2. These stood out as the strongest influences pushing the model to see higher risk, even though your upper-middle family income and full-time study plans offered some positive counterbalance. On a brighter note, your Year 3 school decile ranking matched the average and actually nudged the prediction slightly toward success, more than your Year 1 decile did. Overall, compared to typical admitted students, your academic metrics like the LSAT and GPA were the key areas where you fell short of the profiles that predict bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar hurdles through targeted preparation. I encourage you to focus on boosting your LSAT and strengthening your academic record, perhaps by retaking the test or pursuing post-bac coursework to demonstrate growth. Consider programs that support Black women in law, like LSAT prep scholarships or mentorship networks, to build a stronger profile for your next application. You've got resilience on your side; use this feedback as a roadmap to reapply stronger. Keep pushing forward—you belong in this field.
